case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

batch_record.reaction_description: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate were added to TBAB/EG DES in an oven-dried vial; the reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C for 15 min, achieving full conversion to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition.
batch_record.raw_text: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: [RESCUED_UPSTREAM] Weak upstream kinetic anchor replaced with deterministic thermal-flow rescue anchor for benchmark evaluation; raw upstream remains recorded separately.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Rescued benchmark anchor: evaluate weak upstream with a conservative 3.0x thermal-flow residence-time reduction target rather than accepting the raw weak-model kinetic anchor.

analogies.summary: This process demonstrates a [3 + 2] dipolar cycloaddition reaction conducted in a continuous flow unit (CFU) with automated pumps. The reaction uses DMSO as solvent with diisopropylamine as base, operating at 60°C with a 20-minute residence time at 0.165 mL/min flow rate, achieving 96% yield. The flow process allows for higher temperature operation (60°C vs 20°C in batch) while maintaining excellent yield, with the batch baseline achieving 100% yield at room temperature in the same reaction time. The continuous flow setup enables better temperature control and process intensification, allowing the reaction to proceed efficiently at elevated temperature with only a minimal decrease in yield compared to the batch process.

chemistry.scope_summary: The process shows a wide scope for synthesizing 1-aryl-1,2,3-triazoles from various anilines, including electron-rich and electron-poor substrates, yielding triazoles efficiently in good yields.

optimization_table.key_findings: CuI (10 mol%) with 1,10-phenanthroline (10 mol%) at 60°C for 20 min gave 100% conversion.

full_record.notes: Flow process achieves high yield with optimized conditions, BPR inferred from reactor scheme.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

chemistry.reaction_name: [2 + 2] photocycloaddition followed by thermal electrocyclic cascade
chemistry.bond_formed: C-C bonds in cyclobutane ring and subsequent cascade products
chemistry.scope_summary: Five aryl enamide derivatives tested (phenyl, 3-F, 3-Br, 3-OMe, 4-CF3) with yields of 63-68% for most substrates. The 4-CF3 derivative was resistant to the thermal fragmentation step.

optimization_table.key_findings: MeCN solvent with 0.5 M concentration at 200°C with 5 min residence time gave optimal results

figures.key_information: photochemical reactor setup with dual light sources positioned above a round bottom flask on a heating plate
figures.caption: Figure 3.  One-pot photo/thermal batch reaction. | Figure 4.  Batch/Flow process and substrate scope tested. Yields in parentheses are NMR yields.

Figure 7 . The ease at which this could be purified by filtration represents one of the main benefits of acetonitrile over xylene, which would not be possible without the use of continuous flow technology. This result represents a 24 h productivity of 490 g. In conclusion, we have demonstrated a rare example of an excited-state photochemical reaction successfully daisy-chained with a thermal r | Figure 6.  Daisy-chained PhotoSyn/FlowSyn and pure product collected by filtration. | Figure 7.  Flow schematic for the optimized fully continuous photo/thermal cascade sequence.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low yield (16%) and long reaction time (120 min) in batch mode
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Daisy-chaining photochemistry with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions enabled by flow technology, achieving high productivity
translation_logic.what_changed: Temperature increased to 200°C, residence time reduced to 5 min, back pressure of 34 bar applied
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Solvent (MeCN), concentration (0.5 M), light source power (36 W)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: High temperature thermal cascade reactions safer in flow than batch

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing photochemical setup with light sources
Flow schematic for daisy-chained photo/thermal process

full_record.notes: High confidence extraction with clear quantitative data from optimization tables. Flow process shows dramatic improvement in yield (16% to 88%) and time reduction (120 min to 5 min). Productivity of 20 g/h mentioned in key claim.

analogies.summary: This transition-metal-catalyzed cross-coupling reaction was performed using both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis in acetonitrile solvent with triethylamine as base. The flow process utilized an X-Cube Flash reactor with stainless steel tubing (1.0 mm ID, 4.0 mL volume) operated at 170°C with a 10-minute residence time and 0.4 mL/min flow rate under 10 bar back pressure. Both batch (150°C, 5 hours, microwave heating) and flow conditions achieved 94% yield at 0.65 M concentration. The flow process provided a significant time advantage, reducing reaction time from 5 hours to 10 minutes (30-fold acceleration) while allowing operation at higher temperature (170°C vs 150°C) and eliminating the need for microwave heating. The back pressure regulator was essential for maintaining single-phase liquid conditions at the elevated temperature.

chemistry.scope_summary: Mizoroki–Heck couplings of aryl iodides and bromides with butyl acrylate were successfully performed in both batch/microwave and continuous-flow modes using a homogeneous Pd(OAc)2 catalyst with high yield and selectivity.

optimization_table.key_findings: The Mizoroki–Heck coupling using 0.01 mol % Pd(OAc)2 at 170°C achieved full conversion and high selectivity.

full_record.notes: Back pressure set based on text but supported by reactor scheme figure. No conflicts between text and figures were identified.

steps.name: Batch Conditions
assumptions: Intensification policy active downstream → tau_flow must remain ≤ 15.0 min

steps.name: Kinetics & Residence Time
steps.summary: τ = 5.0 min (council-approved — not re-derived from kinetics). Implied IF = 3× vs batch.
assumptions: τ set by ENGINE council — kinetics estimation bypassed

steps.name: Reactor Sizing
steps.summary: V_R = 5.00 mL, d = 1.00 mm, L = 6.37 m, Pe = 58 (⚠ axial dispersion)
warnings: Pe = 58 < 100 — significant axial dispersion. Reactor behaves closer to CSTR than PFR. Reduce d (smaller d → higher Pe) or accept lower conversion efficiency.

steps.name: Mass Transfer
steps.summary: t_mix = 101.3 s, Da = 5.907 — Transition zone — mixing becoming significant
warnings: Da = 5.91 > 1: mixing may limit conversion. Consider static mixer insert.
assumptions: D = 1e-09 m²/s (typical for small organic molecules in liquid)

steps.name: Heat Transfer
steps.summary: Q_gen = 1.1813 W, Q_rem = 60.0000 W, Da_th = 0.0197 — Thermally safe

steps.name: Back-Pressure Regulator
steps.summary: BPR REQUIRED: 5.0 bar (Gas-liquid system detected — BPR mandatory to maintain gas solubility (Henry's law: C_gas = H·P) and prevent uncontrolled degassing.)
equations: C_{\mathrm{gas}} = H \cdot P_{\mathrm{partial}}\quad\text{(higher BPR} \Rightarrow \text{more dissolved gas)}

steps.name: Process Metrics
steps.summary: STY = 4.9800 mol/(L·h), P = 24.900 mmol/h, ṅ_lim = 0.0333 mmol/min, startup waste = 15.0 mL, IF = 3×

streams.pump_role: Delivering the substrates phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate to the reactor.
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: Both substrates are combined in a single stream as per the defined logic.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: A T-mixer is suitable for combining the substrates before entering the reactor.
post_reactor_steps: Quench with water to stop the reaction.
proposal.chemistry_notes:
The reaction is a 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition between phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate, requiring a quenching step with water to halt the reaction. The flow conditions were set to achieve a residence time of 0.6 min, which is within the calculated range of 0.4–2.2 min. The flow rate of 0.500 mL/min was derived from the total flow rate needed to maintain this residence time in a reactor volume of 0.31 mL. The temperature was set to 120 °C as per the batch conditions, and a BPR of 5.0 bar is required to maintain liquid phase conditions at this temperature.
[RESCUED_UPSTREAM] Council input uses deterministic rescued kinetics for weak-upstream benchmark.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculated to be 0.6 min based on reactor volume and flow rate (0.31 mL / 0.500 mL/min).
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch conditions (120 °C) to ensure full conversion.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Required as per calculations to maintain liquid phase at 120 °C (BPR required > 1).
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: PFA is suitable for high-temperature applications and is chemically resistant.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Set to 0.5 M as per batch conditions.
